Category: --- DESIGN & CONSTRUCTION DOCUMENTS: the drawings, specifications, and models that define what to build ---

Description: Complete package of design documents issued for a project phase — architectural, structural, MEP, civil, landscape sheets organized by discipline and numbered by convention. Issued at milestones (SD, DD, CD, IFC, As-Built). Each set supersedes the prior issue. The authoritative record of design intent.
Industry Standard: AIA E202 (BIM Protocol), AIA G702, NCS (National CAD Standard), US National BIM Standard
Common Values: Schematic Design (SD), Design Development (DD), Construction Documents (CD), Issued for Construction (IFC), Issued for Permit, As-Built, Record Set
Source Systems: Bluebeam, Procore, PlanGrid (Autodesk), Newforma, ProjectWise (Bentley), BIM 360 Docs, SharePoint, Physical plan rooms
Procore Coverage: High — drawing_revision table tracks all revisions with discipline, number, revision number, dates. Widely adopted across projects.
Benchmarking Use: Drawing revision frequency by discipline; revision volume over time; late-stage revision rate (post-IFC revisions signal design instability)
Notes: Drawing sets are the primary REFERENCES target — RFIs, submittals, observations, and punch items all reference specific drawing sheets. VERSIONED_AS relationship tracks revision history. Cross-domain: GOVERNS building elements.

Description: Individual sheet within a drawing set — identified by discipline prefix and sheet number (e.g., S-301, M-201, A-102). Contains plan views, sections, details, schedules, and notes for a specific scope. Sheets carry a revision block tracking issue dates and revision descriptions.
Industry Standard: NCS (sheet numbering convention), AIA CAD Layer Guidelines
Common Values: Architectural (A-series), Structural (S-series), Mechanical (M-series), Electrical (E-series), Plumbing (P-series), Civil (C-series), Landscape (L-series), Fire Protection (FP-series)
Source Systems: Bluebeam, Procore, PlanGrid, AutoCAD, Revit, ProjectWise
Procore Coverage: High — drawing_revision captures individual sheet-level data: discipline, number, revision, received_date, current flag. Sheet-level tracking is core functionality.
Benchmarking Use: Sheets per discipline; revision count per sheet; average revisions per sheet by discipline (structural vs. architectural)
Notes: Sheets are what RFIs reference (drawing_number field on rfis). Most granular unit of design documentation. ATTACHED_TO locations via plan markups.

Description: A new version of a drawing sheet issued to correct errors, incorporate changes, or add information. Tracked by revision number (Rev A, Rev B, or numeric) with a revision date and description of changes. Each revision supersedes the prior version. Revision clouds on the sheet highlight changed areas.
Industry Standard: AIA standard revision tracking, NCS revision block format
Common Values: Rev 0 (original issue), Rev A/B/C or Rev 1/2/3, Addendum revisions, Bulletin revisions, ASI revisions
Source Systems: Bluebeam, Procore, PlanGrid, Newforma, ProjectWise
Procore Coverage: High — drawing_revision table is the primary record. Fields: revision number, revision date, received_date, description, discipline, current (boolean). Well-structured and widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Revision volume by phase; revisions per drawing; post-IFC revision rate; time between revisions
Notes: VERSIONED_AS relationship. Post-IFC revisions almost always generate change orders — high correlation with cost growth. ORIGINATED_FROM design changes, ASIs, or RFI responses.

Description: Written document defining material, product, and installation requirements for a specific scope of work — organized by CSI MasterFormat division and section number. Contains Part 1 (General), Part 2 (Products), Part 3 (Execution). Spec sections are the legal standard for quality; conflicts between specs and drawings generate RFIs.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat (50 divisions), CSI SectionFormat (3-part structure), ASTM referenced standards
Common Values: Division 03 30 00 Cast-in-Place Concrete, Division 05 12 00 Structural Steel, Division 08 44 00 Curtain Walls, Division 09 29 00 Gypsum Board, Division 26 05 00 Common Work Results for Electrical
Source Systems: Specpoint, BSD SpecLink, e-SPEC, Procore, Bluebeam (for review), Word/PDF (authoring)
Procore Coverage: Medium — specification_sections table exists with section number, description, and revision tracking. Used for submittal and RFI cross-referencing. Adoption depends on whether the project team enters spec sections into Procore.
Benchmarking Use: Spec sections per project by division; spec-to-submittal coverage ratio
Notes: GOVERNS materials and installation quality. Submittals are organized by spec section (specification_section_id on submittals). RFIs often reference spec sections. The bridge between design intent and construction quality.

Description: Formal modification to bidding or contract documents issued before contract execution — changes drawings, specs, or bid conditions. Numbered sequentially (Addendum 1, 2, 3). Issued during bidding to address errors, respond to bidder questions, or modify scope. All bidders must acknowledge receipt. After contract execution, changes become ASIs or change orders instead.
Industry Standard: AIA A701 (Instructions to Bidders), AIA A201 (General Conditions §1.1)
Common Values: Addendum 1, 2, 3; Drawing revisions via addendum; Spec section revisions; Bid form modifications; Bid date extensions
Source Systems: Procore (as correspondence or document), Bluebeam, email, physical distribution
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated addendum table. Tracked as correspondence or uploaded documents. Not structured as a distinct record type.
Benchmarking Use: Addendum count per project; addenda issued within 7 days of bid (late addenda correlate with bid errors)
Notes: SUPERSEDES prior bid document versions. Addenda volume during bidding indicates design completeness. High addendum count = design not ready for bidding. BECOMES contract document upon execution.

Description: Post-contract design clarification or minor change issued by the architect that does not change the contract sum or time. ASIs modify drawings or specs to clarify intent, correct minor errors, or adjust details. If the change affects cost or schedule, it should be a change order, not an ASI — disputes over this boundary generate claims.
Industry Standard: AIA G710 (Architect's Supplemental Instructions)
Common Values: ASI-001, ASI-002; Drawing revisions via ASI; Spec clarifications; Detail modifications
Source Systems: Procore (as correspondence or site instruction), Newforma, email, AIA forms
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated ASI table. Typically entered as correspondence or site instruction. Some teams use custom correspondence types.
Benchmarking Use: ASI count per project; ASI-to-change-order conversion rate (ASIs that should have been COs)
Notes: Contentious document type: GCs argue ASIs that affect cost should be change orders. ORIGINATED_FROM design review or field conditions. TRIGGERS drawing revisions. Cost impact disputes are common.

Category: --- REQUESTS & CLARIFICATIONS: formal questions and information exchanges that drive decisions ---

Description: Formal question from the contractor to the design team requesting clarification on drawings, specs, or design intent. RFIs have a structured lifecycle: initiated → assigned → responded → closed/accepted. Each RFI identifies the requesting party, assigned responder (typically architect or engineer of record), drawing/spec reference, due date, and cost/schedule impact flags. The answer becomes a binding project document.
Industry Standard: AIA G716 (Request for Information), ConsensusDocs 200
Common Values: Open, Closed, Draft, Overdue; Cost impact: yes_known, yes_unknown, no_impact, tbd, n_a; Schedule impact: yes/no with days; Priority: Normal, Urgent, Emergency
Source Systems: Procore, PlanGrid, Newforma, Aconex, Submittal Exchange, email (legacy)
Procore Coverage: High — rfis table is one of the most structured and adopted tools. Fields: subject, question, status, cost_impact, schedule_impact, predicted_topic (ML-classified, 176 topics, 91% populated), drawing_number, due_date, due_date_variance, ball_in_court, responsible_contractor, assignees, overdue flag. 17,540+ cos.
Benchmarking Use: RFI volume per project; response time (days); overdue rate; RFI-to-change-order conversion rate; RFI volume by predicted_topic; cost/schedule impact rate
Notes: The strongest structured document signal in Procore. predicted_topic enables zero-NLP topic classification. ORIGINATED_FROM design ambiguity or field conditions. BECOMES change_event when cost impact is confirmed (origin_type = 'Rfi::Header'). REFERENCES drawing sheets and spec sections.

Description: Formal answer to an RFI from the design team — provides the clarification, direction, or decision requested. May include supplementary sketches, revised details, or references to existing drawings. Response status (official vs. preliminary) affects contractual weight. Multiple responses may occur on a single RFI if follow-up is needed.
Industry Standard: AIA G716
Common Values: Official response, Preliminary response, Follow-up response; Response with supplemental sketch; Response referencing ASI
Source Systems: Procore, PlanGrid, Newforma, email
Procore Coverage: High — rfi_responses table tracks each response with text, responder, date, and official status. rfi_assignees tracks who is responsible for responding.
Benchmarking Use: Average response time; response time by assignee (architect vs. engineer); overdue response rate
Notes: RESPONDS_TO the RFI question. Response quality affects downstream work — vague responses generate follow-up RFIs. Late responses delay construction. LINKED_TO drawing revisions when the response triggers a design change.

Description: Formal request from GC to subcontractor or supplier for pricing on a change, additional work, or alternate scope. Not the same as a bid-phase RFP — this is a construction-phase pricing exercise. Includes scope description, affected drawings/specs, and requested response date. The returned proposal becomes a cost input to the change management process.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; follows company/contract procedures
Common Values: RFP for change pricing, T&M request, Unit price request, Alternate pricing
Source Systems: Procore (request_for_quotes table), email, Excel, company-specific forms
Procore Coverage: Medium — request_for_quotes, request_for_quote_quotes, and request_for_quote_responses tables exist and are Ready. Structured with quotes and responses.
Benchmarking Use: RFP turnaround time; number of quotes received per RFP; pricing variance between quotes
Notes: LINKED_TO change events. The response (quote) feeds into change event line items for cost estimation. ASSIGNED_TO subcontractors for their scope. Pricing velocity affects change management cycle time.

Category: --- SUBMITTALS & APPROVALS: product and fabrication data submitted for design team review ---

Description: Formal submission of product data, shop drawings, samples, or other documentation to the design team for review and approval before procurement or installation. Every specified material requires a submittal. Submittals have a structured workflow: created → submitted → under review → approved/rejected/revise & resubmit. Ball-in-court tracking identifies the current responsible party. Submittal titles describe the specific product or material.
Industry Standard: CSI standards, AIA A201 §3.12 (Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples), spec section Part 1.3 (Submittals)
Common Values: Approved, Approved as Noted, Revise and Resubmit, Rejected, Submitted, Under Review, Draft, Closed; Types: Shop Drawing, Product Data, Sample, Test Report, Closeout, O&M Manual
Source Systems: Procore, Submittal Exchange, PlanGrid, Newforma, Aconex, Bluebeam (for review markups)
Procore Coverage: High — submittals table is highly structured and widely adopted. Fields: title (product description), status, specification_section_id, overdue flag, due_date, submitted_date, approved_date, ball_in_court, type, received_date, actual_delivery_date (1% populated), required_on_site_date (6% populated). Companion tables: submittal_approvers, submittal_ball_in_courts, submittal_distributions. 15K+ cos with overdue data.
Benchmarking Use: Submittal volume per project; approval cycle time; overdue rate; revise-and-resubmit rate; submittals per spec section; approval time by reviewer
Notes: The primary material and product documentation pathway. Submittal titles are the strongest material signal in Procore — every material is specified through a submittal. REFERENCES spec sections (specification_section_id). PRECEDES procurement and installation. Overdue submittals are a leading indicator of material delays.

Description: The design team's review action on a submittal — approve, approve as noted, reject, or revise and resubmit. Reviewer markups on shop drawings are binding design modifications. 'Approved as Noted' is the most common disposition — the notes become part of the contract documents. The review cycle (submit → review → resubmit if needed → re-review) can take 2-6 weeks per round.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §4.2.7 (Review of Submittals)
Common Values: Approved, Approved as Noted, Rejected, Revise and Resubmit, No Exception Taken, Make Corrections Noted
Source Systems: Procore, Submittal Exchange, Bluebeam, Newforma
Procore Coverage: High — submittal_approvers table tracks each reviewer's action, response date, and approval status. submittal_ball_in_courts tracks current responsible party.
Benchmarking Use: Review cycle time (submit to approval); rejection rate; resubmittal rate; reviewer response time
Notes: RESPONDS_TO the submittal. Review duration is a key schedule metric — slow reviews delay procurement. Multiple reviewer chains (sub → GC → architect → engineer) compound delay. APPROVED_AS status drives procurement release.

Description: A subcontractor-prepared or fabricator-prepared detailed drawing showing dimensions, connections, materials, and fabrication details for their specific scope — submitted for design team review. Shop drawings translate design intent into constructable details. Steel connections, curtain wall anchoring, precast panel layouts, and MEP coordination drawings are among the most complex and schedule-critical.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §3.12, AISC Code of Standard Practice (steel), PCI (precast)
Common Values: Structural steel connection details, Curtain wall anchor layouts, Precast panel elevations, Mechanical coordination drawings, Electrical riser diagrams, Plumbing isometrics
Source Systems: Procore (as submittal), Bluebeam, SDS/2 (steel), AutoCAD/Revit (subcontractor), Tekla
Procore Coverage: High — tracked as submittal records in Procore. Shop drawing submittals are among the highest-volume and most tracked. Title field identifies the scope.
Benchmarking Use: Shop drawing turnaround time by trade; revision count; approval cycle length for structural vs. MEP vs. architectural
Notes: TYPED_AS a submittal. Shop drawings are where design errors surface — discrepancies between design drawings and shop drawings generate RFIs. Steel shop drawings are typically critical path. DESIGNED_BY subcontractor/fabricator, APPROVED_AS by architect/engineer.

Description: Manufacturer's published literature, cut sheets, performance data, and test reports submitted to demonstrate that a specific product meets specification requirements. Less complex than shop drawings but higher volume — every specified product needs a product data submittal. Submittal includes model numbers, finishes, dimensions, and performance ratings.
Industry Standard: CSI submittals classification, ASTM/UL/FM test standards referenced in specs
Common Values: Fixture cut sheets, Equipment performance data, Material safety data sheets (SDS), Manufacturer warranties, Test/certification reports
Source Systems: Procore (as submittal), Submittal Exchange, Newforma, manufacturer websites
Procore Coverage: High — tracked as submittal records. Product data submittals are the highest-volume submittal type on most projects.
Benchmarking Use: Product data submittal volume; cycle time vs. shop drawings; rejection rate by spec section
Notes: TYPED_AS a submittal. Product data drives the procurement pipeline — approved product data triggers purchase orders. SPECIFIES the exact product that will be installed. REFERENCES spec sections for compliance.

Description: Physical material sample submitted for design team review and approval — colors, textures, finishes, and material quality. Includes mockup panels, paint samples, flooring tiles, stone selections, hardware finishes. Physical samples must be delivered and reviewed in person, adding logistics complexity. Approved samples become the standard for field acceptance.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §3.12 (Samples)
Common Values: Paint color samples, Flooring samples, Stone/tile samples, Hardware finish samples, Curtain wall mockup, Brick/masonry sample panel
Source Systems: Procore (as submittal), physical delivery + in-person review
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked as submittal records in Procore, but the physical sample review component is outside the system. Approval status is captured; the physical sample itself is not.
Benchmarking Use: Sample submittal count per project; sample approval cycle time; sample-to-installation lead time
Notes: TYPED_AS a submittal. Samples are the quality standard — field work is accepted or rejected based on approved sample. Mockup panels for exterior enclosure are expensive and schedule-critical. GOVERNS installation quality for visual trades (paint, tile, masonry).

Category: --- CORRESPONDENCE & NOTICES: formal communications between project parties ---

Description: Formal written communication between project parties — letters, emails tracked as project records, and notices that require acknowledgment or response. Correspondence types are configurable per project (letters, notices, memos, transmittals). Each has sender, recipient(s), date, subject, and response tracking. Used for contractual notifications, design directives, schedule notices, and claims documentation.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (various notice provisions), contract-specific notice requirements
Common Values: Letter, Notice, Memo, Transmittal, Email (tracked), Owner Directive, Claim Notice, Delay Notice, Change Directive
Source Systems: Procore, Newforma, Aconex, email (unstructured), physical mail
Procore Coverage: High — correspondences table with structured type (via correspondence_types), status, assignees, responses. correspondence_responses tracks reply chain. correspondence_types defines the taxonomy per project. Ready for import.
Benchmarking Use: Correspondence volume by type; response time; notice frequency (delay notices, change directives)
Notes: Correspondence is the contractual communication backbone. Delay notices, claim notices, and change directives have legal significance. DOCUMENTS project decisions and directives. ASSIGNED_TO responsible parties for response. Types are configurable per project — cross-project normalization required.

Description: Cover document accompanying a package of drawings, specifications, submittals, or other documents sent between parties. Records what was sent, to whom, when, and for what purpose (for review, for approval, for information, for construction). The transmittal itself is not the content — it's the record of transmission. Increasingly replaced by digital distribution but still required on many projects.
Industry Standard: AIA G810 (Transmittal Letter)
Common Values: For Review, For Approval, For Information, For Construction, For Record, As Requested
Source Systems: Procore (as correspondence type), Newforma, Bluebeam, Aconex, paper forms
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked as a correspondence type in Procore. Not a separate table — uses the correspondences framework with a 'Transmittal' type.
Benchmarking Use: Transmittal volume; documents per transmittal; transmittal acknowledgment time
Notes: CONTAINS document references. The metadata layer on document distribution. Being replaced by digital distribution (Procore's built-in document sharing) but still contractually required on many projects.

Description: Formal written notification that a delay event has occurred or is anticipated — required by most contracts within a specified timeframe (often 7-14 days) to preserve delay claim rights. Must identify the cause, affected activities, anticipated duration, and responsible party. Failure to provide timely notice can waive the right to a time extension or delay damages.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §15.1.6 (Claims for Additional Time), ConsensusDocs 200 §6.3
Common Values: Weather delay notice, Material delay notice, Owner-caused delay notice, Design delay notice, Force majeure notice, Differing conditions notice
Source Systems: Procore (as correspondence), email, AIA forms, contract-specific forms
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated delay notice table. Tracked as correspondence with a 'Notice' or custom type. The structured delay data lives in daily_log_delay, not in the notice itself.
Benchmarking Use: Delay notice frequency; notice-to-time-extension conversion rate; average days between event and notice
Notes: Contractually critical — late notice can waive claims. TRIGGERED_BY delay events (daily_log_delay). BECOMES basis for time extension requests and change orders. REFERENCES schedule activities and delay analysis.

Description: Owner-issued directive requiring the contractor to proceed with changed work before the cost and time impact is agreed upon. Used when urgency prevents waiting for a negotiated change order. The contractor must proceed but preserves the right to dispute cost and time later. This is NOT a change order — it's a unilateral directive.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §7.3 (Construction Change Directives), AIA G714
Common Values: CCD-001, CCD-002; Proceed immediately; Cost to be determined; Time impact TBD
Source Systems: Procore (as correspondence or site instruction), AIA forms, email
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated CCD table. Typically entered as correspondence, site instruction, or tracked in change events. Cost implications flow through change_events.
Benchmarking Use: CCD frequency; CCD-to-change-order resolution time; disputed CCD rate
Notes: Contentious document — contractor must comply but may dispute cost. TRIGGERS change events. ORIGINATED_FROM owner or design team. BECOMES negotiated change order when cost/time is agreed. The gap between CCD issuance and CO agreement is where disputes live.

Category: --- MEETING RECORDS: formal records of project decisions and commitments ---

Description: Formally scheduled project meeting with defined attendees, agenda, and documented outcomes. Meetings are the primary decision-making forum on construction projects. Common types: OAC (Owner-Architect-Contractor), progress, coordination, safety, preconstruction. Meeting frequency and attendee mix vary by project phase.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; governed by contract and project-specific requirements
Common Values: OAC Meeting, Progress Meeting, Coordination Meeting, Safety Meeting, Preconstruction Meeting, Subcontractor Meeting, Scheduling Meeting, Closeout Meeting
Source Systems: Procore, Microsoft Teams/Outlook, Google Calendar, Newforma, paper minutes
Procore Coverage: High — meeting table tracks meeting records with title, date, location, status, attendees. meeting_attendee tracks participation. Structured and widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Meeting frequency by type; attendee count; action item generation rate per meeting
Notes: Meetings are where DOCUMENTS relationship is strongest — meeting minutes document decisions, commitments, and action items. CONTAINS meeting items (agenda/action items). Regular meeting cadence is a project health indicator.

Description: Individual agenda item or action item within a meeting — captures topic, discussion, responsible party, due date, and status. Meeting items carry forward between meetings (rolling action item log). Items can be categorized by topic (schedule, budget, safety, quality, design). The meeting minutes document is the formal record of what was discussed and decided.
Industry Standard: AIA G712 (Project Meeting Minutes form)
Common Values: Open, Closed, In Progress, Carried Forward; Categories: Schedule, Budget, Design, Safety, Quality, Closeout, General; Priority: Normal, High, Critical
Source Systems: Procore, Microsoft Word, Newforma, meeting-specific apps
Procore Coverage: High — meeting_item table tracks individual items with description, status, assignees (meeting_item_assignee), due dates, and category. Items carry forward across meetings. Well-structured.
Benchmarking Use: Open action items per meeting; action item aging; closure rate; items by category
Notes: ASSIGNED_TO responsible parties for action. Action items that stay open across multiple meetings signal blocked issues. LINKED_TO other project records (RFIs, submittals, change events) when items reference them. Meeting minutes are admissible as project records in disputes.

Category: --- FIELD COMMUNICATIONS: information created and exchanged during daily construction operations ---

Description: Comprehensive daily site record compiled by the superintendent or project engineer — combines weather, manpower, equipment, deliveries, visitors, delays, notes, safety violations, and work performed into a single daily project narrative. The daily report is the legal record of what happened on site each day. In a dispute, daily reports are the most-referenced project document.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; contractually required on most projects; OSHA recordkeeping requirements for safety events
Common Values: One report per project per day; sections: Weather, Manpower, Equipment, Deliveries, Delays, Notes, Safety, Visitors, Scheduled Work, Productivity
Source Systems: Procore (20+ daily_log_* tables), Raken, FieldWire, Excel, paper forms
Procore Coverage: High — Procore has the most comprehensive daily log system in the industry: daily_log_note, daily_log_manpower, daily_log_equipment, daily_log_delivery, daily_log_delay, daily_log_safety_violation, daily_log_visitor, daily_log_waste, daily_log_phone_call, daily_log_plan_revision, daily_log_scheduled_work, daily_log_productivity, daily_log_observed_weather_condition, daily_log_construction_reports. All Ready.
Benchmarking Use: Daily log completion rate; entries per log section; projects with consistent daily logging; delay days logged per project
Notes: The richest daily data source in construction. DOCUMENTS field activity for every project day. daily_log_delay.delay_type is a structured enum (Weather, Material, Safety, etc.). daily_log_manpower.trade_name is the most direct trade signal. Cross-domain: feeds schedule variance, safety tracking, and productivity analysis.

Description: Free-text field observation entered as part of the daily log — captures site conditions, work progress, issues, coordination problems, or any noteworthy event that doesn't fit structured log categories. Notes may be flagged as issue days. Attachments (photos) can be linked. Notes are the unstructured narrative complement to the structured log sections.
Industry Standard: No standard; company/project specific
Common Values: Free-text observations; is_issue_day flag; photo attachments; keyword-rich descriptions of site conditions
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_note), Raken, paper logs
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_note table with comment field, is_issue_day flag, attachments. Widely used. ~130K+ Tier 1 records for keyword-based signals (rework, shortage, etc.).
Benchmarking Use: Note volume per project; issue-day frequency; keyword hit rates for specific topics (rework, material shortage, design issue)
Notes: Primary NLP signal source. Keywords in daily log notes identify rework (~130K records), material shortages (0.09% hit rate for 'shortage'), design issues, and schedule concerns. DOCUMENTS daily field conditions. Low structured value but high NLP potential.

Description: Written directive from the GC superintendent or project manager to a subcontractor or crew — directs specific field action. Site instructions address means and methods, sequence changes, safety corrections, or quality requirements. They are NOT design changes (those come from the architect via ASI). Site instructions are GC-to-sub directives.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; governed by subcontract terms
Common Values: Directive to correct work, Sequence change, Safety correction, Access restriction, Work area assignment, Temporary protection requirement
Source Systems: Procore (site_instructions table), paper forms, email
Procore Coverage: Medium — site_instructions table exists and is Ready. Has title, description, status, assignees, distribution list, cost impact fields. Less widely adopted than RFIs or submittals but structured when used.
Benchmarking Use: Site instruction volume per project; instruction-to-change-order rate; instructions by trade
Notes: ASSIGNED_TO subcontractors. ORIGINATED_FROM field conditions, safety concerns, or coordination issues. If a site instruction changes scope, it may TRIGGER a change event. Important for documenting GC directives vs. design team directives.

Description: Record of field phone calls tracked as part of the daily log — documents who called whom, subject, and outcome. Captures communications that don't have a formal paper trail. Historically important for documenting verbal directions and commitments on construction sites.
Industry Standard: No standard
Common Values: Caller, recipient, subject, notes, date/time
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_phone_call), paper logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_phone_call table exists and is Ready. Structured fields for caller, recipient, subject, notes. Adoption varies — not all projects track phone calls.
Benchmarking Use: Phone call log volume; calls per day; percentage of projects using phone call tracking
Notes: Declining in relevance as text/email replaces phone calls. Still used on some projects to document verbal directions. DOCUMENTS verbal communications that may have contractual significance.

Description: Field-level record that a new drawing revision has been received and acknowledged on the jobsite. Ensures the construction team is working from current documents. Tracked in the daily log as a notification event — which drawings were updated, when received, and who was notified.
Industry Standard: No standard; best practice for document control
Common Values: Drawing number, revision number, date received, distributed to
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_plan_revision), field document control logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_plan_revision table exists and is Ready. Captures the field receipt of drawing revisions. Links to drawing_revision records.
Benchmarking Use: Plan revision receipt frequency; time from issuance to field receipt; drawings updated per revision notice
Notes: LINKED_TO drawing_revision records. Ensures field teams are working from current documents. Late distribution of revised drawings is a common cause of rework. FOLLOWS the drawing revision event.

Category: --- ACTION PLANS & TASKS: structured work assignments and tracking ---

Description: Structured set of tasks with assignments, due dates, and completion tracking — used for punch list management, commissioning tasks, closeout activities, safety action items, or any coordinated work sequence. Action plans have approval workflows and completion notifications. Each plan contains line items (tasks) that are individually assigned and tracked.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; project-specific
Common Values: Punch List Action Plan, Commissioning Action Plan, Safety Corrective Action Plan, Closeout Action Plan, Pre-Occupancy Action Plan
Source Systems: Procore (action_plan, action_plan_line_item tables), Excel, Microsoft Planner, Asana
Procore Coverage: High — action_plan and action_plan_line_item tables are structured with approvers, assignees, status, due dates, references to other records, and completion tracking. Custom fields supported. Companion tables: action_plan_approver, action_plan_completed_receiver, action_plan_line_item_assignee, action_plan_line_item_record, action_plan_line_item_reference.
Benchmarking Use: Action plan count per project; task completion rate; overdue task rate; average days to complete
Notes: CONTAINS action plan line items. Action plans are the execution layer that converts identified issues (observations, punch items, inspection deficiencies) into tracked remediation work. LINKED_TO other records via action_plan_line_item_record and action_plan_line_item_reference.

Description: Individual task within an action plan — a specific work item assigned to a responsible party with a due date and status. Tasks can reference other project records (observations, punch items, RFIs) and have their own assignees distinct from the plan-level assignment. Completion requires sign-off per the plan's approval workflow.
Industry Standard: No standard
Common Values: Not Started, In Progress, Complete, Blocked; Priority: Normal, High, Urgent
Source Systems: Procore (action_plan_line_item), Excel task lists, project management tools
Procore Coverage: High — action_plan_line_item table with status, assignees, due dates, and cross-references to other records. action_plan_line_item_assignee tracks responsibility. action_plan_line_item_record links to source issues.
Benchmarking Use: Task completion rate; average days to complete; overdue rate; tasks per assignee
Notes: ASSIGNED_TO responsible parties. LINKED_TO source records (the observation, punch item, or deficiency that created the task). The task is the atomic unit of tracked remediation work.

Description: General-purpose task tracked at the project level — not tied to an action plan. Used for ad-hoc work items, reminders, and assignments that don't fit the formal action plan structure. Lighter weight than action plan tasks but less structured.
Industry Standard: No standard
Common Values: Open, Complete; Assigned to individual; Due date
Source Systems: Procore (task table), email reminders, personal task managers
Procore Coverage: Medium — task table exists and is Ready. Simple structure with status, assignee, due date, and description. Less adoption than action plans.
Benchmarking Use: Task volume per project; completion rate; overdue rate
Notes: Simpler than action plan line items. Used for ad-hoc coordination. Less cross-referencing to other project records.

Category: --- VISUAL DOCUMENTATION: photos, videos, and visual records of site conditions ---

Description: Photograph documenting site conditions, work progress, quality issues, safety hazards, or installed work. Photos are attached to locations, observations, inspections, daily logs, and punch items. Geotagged and timestamped photos are increasingly used as legal evidence of conditions. Photo management is one of the highest-volume documentation activities on a project.
Industry Standard: No standard; ASTM E1020 (Standard Practice for Reporting Photographic Documentation)
Common Values: Progress photos, Quality documentation, Safety hazard, Pre-existing condition, Punch item photo, Inspection photo, Delivery photo, Weather condition
Source Systems: Procore (photo table), PlanGrid, OpenSpace, HoloBuilder, smartphone cameras, drones
Procore Coverage: High — photo table is structured with title, description, location, date, trade, category. Photos can be attached to observations, punch items, inspections, and daily logs via attachment tables. Very widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Photo volume per project; photos per day; photos by category; percentage of observations with photos
Notes: ATTACHED_TO nearly every other document type (observations, punch items, inspections, daily logs). Photos are the visual evidence layer — they prove conditions at a point in time. LOCATED_AT specific locations. DOCUMENTS field conditions.

Description: Video recording of site conditions, work-in-progress, or safety events. Increasingly used for progress documentation (time-lapse), drone flyovers, quality recording (concrete pours, waterproofing installation), and safety incident documentation. 360-degree video enables virtual site walks.
Industry Standard: No standard
Common Values: Progress time-lapse, Drone flyover, Work-in-progress recording, Safety event recording, Quality documentation, Pre-existing condition survey
Source Systems: Procore (as photo/attachment), OpenSpace, Matterport, DroneDeploy, HoloBuilder, smartphone video
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated video table. Videos stored as attachments to photos, observations, or daily logs. No structured metadata specific to video (duration, camera location, etc.).
Benchmarking Use: Video documentation frequency; percentage of projects with drone documentation
Notes: Growing in importance with drone and 360-camera adoption. DOCUMENTS conditions with richer context than photos. CAPTURED_BY drones, 360 cameras, or body cameras. Storage and retrieval at scale remains a challenge.

Category: --- REGULATORY & COMPLIANCE DOCUMENTS: records required by authorities and regulations ---

Description: Documentation submitted to and received from regulatory authorities — building permit applications, permit issuance records, permit conditions, and inspection cards. Tracks permit status from application through issuance. Critical path item — construction cannot start without a building permit.
Industry Standard: IBC (International Building Code), local building codes, local permit application requirements
Common Values: Applied, Under Review, Approved, Issued, Conditional, Expired; Permit types: Building, Electrical, Plumbing, Mechanical, Fire Protection, Demolition
Source Systems: Procore (as form or document), city/county permitting portals, paper applications
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated permit table. Permits tracked as forms, documents, or custom fields on projects. The permit itself lives in the jurisdiction's system, not in Procore.
Benchmarking Use: Permit application-to-issuance time; permit revision count; conditional permit frequency
Notes: DEPENDS_ON design document completion. ENABLES construction activities. Permit conditions may restrict work hours, noise, traffic, or require specific inspections. GOVERNS what can be built. Cross-domain: LINKED_TO phases (permitting phase) and regulatory authorities.

Description: Proof of insurance coverage provided by a subcontractor, vendor, or other project party — verifies they meet the contractual insurance requirements (general liability, workers' comp, auto, umbrella, builders risk). COI collection and compliance tracking is a major administrative task on every project. Missing or expired COIs can prevent a sub from working on site.
Industry Standard: ACORD 25 (Certificate of Liability Insurance), ACORD 28 (Evidence of Property Insurance)
Common Values: General Liability, Workers Compensation, Auto Liability, Umbrella/Excess, Builders Risk, Professional Liability; Status: Compliant, Non-Compliant, Expiring, Expired
Source Systems: Procore (project_vendor_insurances, company_vendor_insurances), myCOI, PINS, BCS, Excel tracking
Procore Coverage: High — project_vendor_insurances and company_vendor_insurances tables in core schema. Structured insurance tracking with policy types, limits, and expiration dates. Compliance status tracking.
Benchmarking Use: COI compliance rate; expired COI count; average days to obtain compliant COI; vendors with missing coverage
Notes: REQUIRED_BY contract terms. COI compliance is a precondition for site access. LINKED_TO vendors/subcontractors. Expired COIs are a risk signal — working without proper insurance exposes the GC and owner to liability.

Description: Legal document in which a contractor, subcontractor, or supplier waives their right to file a mechanic's lien against the property — exchanged with payment. Four types: conditional progress, unconditional progress, conditional final, unconditional final. Collection from all tiers is required before owner makes final payment. Statutory forms required in many states.
Industry Standard: State-specific lien waiver statutes (e.g., California Civil Code §8132-8138), AIA G706/G706A
Common Values: Conditional Progress, Unconditional Progress, Conditional Final, Unconditional Final; Status: Requested, Submitted, Accepted
Source Systems: Procore (payments_lien_waivers, payments_subtier_waivers), DocuSign, paper forms
Procore Coverage: High — payments_lien_waivers and payments_subtier_waivers tables. Structured lien waiver tracking integrated with payment workflow. Widely adopted where payment tools are active.
Benchmarking Use: Lien waiver collection rate; outstanding waivers at closeout; days from payment to waiver receipt
Notes: BILLED_AGAINST payments — exchanged with each payment cycle. Missing lien waivers can block final payment and retainage release. A major closeout bottleneck. LINKED_TO payment instruments and vendor contracts.

Description: Project-specific safety plan defining hazard controls, emergency procedures, PPE requirements, and safety protocols. Required by OSHA and most contracts. Includes job hazard analyses (JHAs), activity hazard analyses (AHAs), emergency action plans, and fall protection plans. Reviewed and updated throughout construction as conditions change.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (Construction Safety Standards), ANSI/ASSP Z590.3 (Prevention through Design)
Common Values: Site-Specific Safety Plan, Job Hazard Analysis (JHA), Activity Hazard Analysis (AHA), Fall Protection Plan, Emergency Action Plan, Crane Lift Plan, Confined Space Entry Plan
Source Systems: Procore (as document/form), Safety management platforms (iAuditor, SafetyCulture), Excel, paper
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated safety plan table. Safety plans uploaded as documents or forms. Safety observations and violations are structured (observation, daily_log_safety_violation) but the governing safety plan document is not.
Benchmarking Use: Safety plan update frequency; JHA coverage (% of high-risk activities with JHAs)
Notes: GOVERNS safety procedures. The safety plan is the standard against which safety observations and violations are measured. COMPLIES_WITH OSHA regulations. Safety documentation requirements vary by owner and jurisdiction.

Description: Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan and related environmental compliance documentation — required by EPA and state environmental agencies for construction sites disturbing 1+ acre of soil. Documents erosion and sediment control measures, inspection schedules, and corrective actions. Updated throughout construction as site conditions change.
Industry Standard: EPA NPDES Construction General Permit, Clean Water Act §402, state stormwater programs
Common Values: SWPPP, Erosion Control Plan, Spill Prevention Plan, Dust Control Plan, Dewatering Plan, Environmental Monitoring Reports
Source Systems: Procore (as document), specialized environmental compliance software, Excel, regulatory agency portals
Procore Coverage: None — no dedicated environmental compliance table. SWPPPs and environmental docs are uploaded as project documents. Inspections may be tracked through the inspections tool but the compliance documents themselves are not structured.
Benchmarking Use: SWPPP inspection frequency; corrective action count; environmental violation rate
Notes: GOVERNS environmental compliance on site. COMPLIES_WITH EPA and state environmental regulations. Regular inspections required (typically weekly + after rain events). Non-compliance carries significant fines. LINKED_TO daily_log_observed_weather_condition for rain events.

Category: --- CLOSEOUT DOCUMENTS: records required to complete and hand over a project ---

Description: Formal list of deficiencies and incomplete work items that must be corrected before the owner accepts the project — compiled during substantial completion walkthrough. While individual punch items are tracked as Quality & Safety Records, the punch list as a compiled document is a contractual deliverable. The list is walked with the owner, architect, and GC.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §9.8 (Substantial Completion), AIA G704 (Certificate of Substantial Completion)
Common Values: Combined punch list document; organized by location, trade, or priority; includes photos and descriptions; sign-off when complete
Source Systems: Procore (punch_item table compiled into list), Excel, PlanGrid, Fieldwire, paper lists
Procore Coverage: High — punch_item records compile into the punch list document. Procore generates punch list reports from structured data (name, location, assignee, status, photos). 11,722+ cos with punch item data.
Benchmarking Use: Punch list length at substantial completion; items per unit/SF; closure rate; days to close all items
Notes: The punch list document is a COMPILATION of individual punch item records. PRECEDES certificate of substantial completion. Punch list closure is a contract milestone. Cross-domain: items map to building elements, trades, and locations.

Description: Marked-up drawings and records showing the actual constructed conditions — deviations from design documents, field modifications, and final installed locations of all systems. As-built documentation is a contractual closeout deliverable. Each subcontractor marks up their scope; the GC compiles into the as-built set. Increasingly digital (BIM as-built models).
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §3.11 (Documents at the Site), contract-specific as-built requirements
Common Values: As-built drawings (redline markups), Record drawings (architect-revised from as-builts), BIM as-built model, Utility location records
Source Systems: Procore (as documents/drawings), Bluebeam, PlanGrid, Revit (BIM as-built), AutoCAD
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated as-built table. As-built drawings uploaded as drawing revisions or documents. The as-built markup process happens in Bluebeam or on paper, then is uploaded to Procore as the final record.
Benchmarking Use: As-built completion rate by trade; days from substantial completion to as-built delivery; percentage of projects with as-built documentation in Procore
Notes: As-built documentation is a major closeout bottleneck — subcontractors are slow to provide markups. DOCUMENTS final constructed conditions. VERSIONED_AS the final drawing revision. REFERENCES drawing sheets (the marked-up versions). Critical for facility operations and future renovations.

Description: Compiled documentation of all installed equipment and systems — manufacturer manuals, maintenance schedules, warranty information, spare parts lists, and operating procedures. Required closeout deliverable for building handover. Organized by building system (HVAC, electrical, plumbing, fire protection, etc.). The owner's reference for facility operations.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §9.10 (Final Completion), spec section 01 78 00 (Closeout Submittals)
Common Values: Equipment O&M manuals, Maintenance schedules, Warranty certificates, Spare parts lists, System operating procedures, Emergency procedures
Source Systems: Procore (as submittals — closeout type), physical binders, digital asset management systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — O&M manuals are submitted through the submittal workflow as closeout submittals. No dedicated O&M table, but the submittal structure captures the approval workflow. Content lives in uploaded documents.
Benchmarking Use: O&M submission rate by trade; days from substantial completion to O&M delivery; completeness by system
Notes: TYPED_AS closeout submittals. O&M manuals are the owner's primary reference for facility operations. Incomplete O&M documentation is a common punchlist and retainage holdback item. REFERENCES building elements and equipment.

Description: Written guarantees from manufacturers, subcontractors, and the GC covering defects in materials and workmanship for a specified period after substantial completion. Standard warranty periods: 1 year (general), 2 years (mechanical/electrical), 5-20 years (roofing), 10 years (structural). Warranty start date is typically the date of substantial completion.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §3.5 (Warranty), spec section 01 78 36 (Warranties), manufacturer-specific warranty terms
Common Values: 1-year general warranty, 2-year MEP warranty, 5-year roofing membrane warranty, 10-year structural warranty, 20-year roofing system warranty, Manufacturer equipment warranties
Source Systems: Procore (as submittals or documents), contract files, manufacturer portals
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated warranty table. Warranties tracked as closeout submittals or uploaded documents. Warranty period tracking and expiration notifications are not natively structured.
Benchmarking Use: Warranty documentation completeness; warranty claims filed; days from issue to warranty claim resolution
Notes: GOVERNS post-completion quality obligations. Warranty claims TRIGGERED_BY defects found during the warranty period. LINKED_TO building elements and equipment. Warranty documentation is often the last closeout item collected. Missing warranties can hold retainage.

Category: --- FORMS & TEMPLATES: configurable structured data collection ---

Description: Configurable structured form for data collection — checklists, inspection templates, safety forms, quality records, or any project-specific data collection need. Forms have defined fields (text, select, date, number, checkbox) with validation rules and workflow routing. Procore's form system (including ProForma/Jira Forms integration) enables project teams to create custom structured data capture without code.
Industry Standard: No industry standard for form structure; content governed by applicable standards (OSHA, AIA, contract)
Common Values: Safety checklists, Quality inspection forms, Pre-task plans, Hot work permits, Concrete pour cards, Commissioning checklists, Material receiving forms, Daily pre-shift safety forms
Source Systems: Procore (form table, ProForma forms API), GoCanvas, iAuditor/SafetyCulture, PlanGrid, Excel, paper forms
Procore Coverage: High — form table exists and is Ready. ProForma forms provide structured field-level data with question IDs, answer types, and Jira field linking. Custom field configs and custom fields supported. Highly flexible.
Benchmarking Use: Form submission volume; form completion rate; forms per project by type; most-used form templates
Notes: Forms are the extensibility layer — they capture structured data that doesn't fit standard tools. DOCUMENTS any field activity or compliance check. ProForma forms bridge to Jira for workflow integration. The form system is how Procore customers create project-specific data capture beyond the built-in tools.

Description: Configurable template defining which daily log sections are required for a project — determines which daily log tables are populated. Template configuration controls whether manpower, equipment, deliveries, delays, visitors, safety violations, and other sections are mandatory, optional, or hidden. Template standardization across a company ensures consistent daily reporting.
Industry Standard: No standard; company/project configuration
Common Values: Required sections, Optional sections, Hidden sections; Configurable by project or company; Custom fields per section
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_construction_reports configuration), company templates
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_construction_reports table tracks report completion and configuration. Template structure is a configuration rather than a document, but it governs data collection consistency.
Benchmarking Use: Template adoption rate; section completion rate by template; companies with standardized templates vs. ad-hoc
Notes: GOVERNS daily log data collection. Template standardization is the key to consistent daily data — projects without standardized templates have sporadic data in optional sections. Cross-company benchmarking requires template normalization.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from document and communication performance ---

Description: Average elapsed time from RFI issuance to final response — measures design team responsiveness and information flow efficiency. Formula: Response Date − Issue Date (in calendar or business days). Long RFI response times are a leading indicator of schedule delays and design coordination problems. Can be segmented by discipline, responsible party, and project phase.
Industry Standard: CII benchmarking metrics; AIA response time provisions in contract
Common Values: Typical range: 3–14 calendar days; > 21 days is a red flag
Source Systems: Derived from: rfis (created_at, due_date), rfi_responses (created_at)
Procore Coverage: High — rfis and rfi_responses tables with timestamps
Benchmarking Use: Compare RFI response time by discipline and project type to benchmark design team responsiveness
Notes: Ball-in-court tracking (rfis_bic) provides granular view of where time is spent. Response time is affected by RFI quality — vague RFIs take longer.

Description: Total RFI count relative to project size — measures design completeness and coordination quality. Formula: Total RFIs / Project Value (per $M) or Total RFIs / Project SF. High RFI density suggests incomplete design documents or poor coordination. Can be segmented by discipline and project phase.
Industry Standard: CII benchmarking; industry typical ranges
Common Values: Typical: 50–300 RFIs per $100M project; varies widely by complexity and delivery method
Source Systems: Derived from: rfis (count), projects (total_value)
Procore Coverage: High — rfis table with structured subject, status, schedule_impact, predicted_topic
Benchmarking Use: Compare RFI density across project types and delivery methods to identify design quality patterns
Notes: RFI predicted_topic (176 ML-classified topics) enables subject-matter breakdown without NLP. High RFI volume in a discipline signals design gaps in that system.

Description: Average elapsed time from submittal submission to final approval — measures the review and approval efficiency for product data and shop drawings. Formula: Approval Date − Submission Date (in business days). Includes resubmission cycles. Long cycle times delay procurement and fabrication.
Industry Standard: CSI Practice Guide for Submittals; contract-specified review periods (typically 10–14 business days)
Common Values: Typical: 10–21 business days first review; resubmittals add 7–14 days each cycle
Source Systems: Derived from: submittals (created_at, approval dates), submittal_approvers (response dates)
Procore Coverage: High — submittals table with status tracking, overdue flag, and approval workflow
Benchmarking Use: Compare submittal cycle time by type and discipline to identify approval bottlenecks
Notes: Overdue submittals (submittals.overdue flag) are a direct material delay risk signal. Cycle time × number of resubmissions = total approval duration.

Description: Percentage of submittals rejected or returned for revision on first review — measures submittal preparation quality and design coordination. Formula: (Rejected + Revise & Resubmit) / Total Submittals × 100. High rejection rate increases cycle time and delays procurement.
Industry Standard: CSI submittal review categories; AIA review stamps
Common Values: Typical: 15–30% rejection/revision rate; > 40% indicates systemic quality issues
Source Systems: Derived from: submittals (status), submittal_approvers (response status)
Procore Coverage: High — submittals table with structured status values
Benchmarking Use: Compare rejection rate by trade and discipline to identify vendors with submittal quality issues
Notes: Rejection rate by trade reveals which subcontractors need submittal preparation support. Resubmission count per submittal tracks rework in the documentation process.

Description: Average number of revisions per drawing sheet — measures design stability and change volume. Formula: Total Revisions / Total Drawing Sheets. Late-stage revisions (post-IFC) are a stronger signal of design instability than early-stage revisions. Can be segmented by discipline.
Industry Standard: NCS revision numbering conventions
Common Values: Typical: 2–5 revisions per sheet over project life; > 3 post-IFC revisions is a red flag
Source Systems: Derived from: drawing_revision (revision_number, discipline, dates)
Procore Coverage: High — drawing_revision table tracks all revisions with discipline and dates
Benchmarking Use: Compare revision frequency by discipline and project phase to identify design instability
Notes: Post-IFC revisions are the most impactful — they drive RFIs, change orders, and rework. Revision frequency by discipline reveals which systems have the most design churn.

Description: Percentage of working days with a completed daily log entry — measures field reporting consistency. Formula: Days with Daily Log / Total Working Days × 100. Consistent daily logging is a prerequisite for field data quality. Low completion rates mean gaps in workforce, weather, delivery, and delay tracking.
Industry Standard: Company-specific daily reporting requirements
Common Values: Typical target: 95–100% of working days; < 80% indicates reporting discipline issues
Source Systems: Derived from: daily_log_construction_reports (log_date), projects (date range)
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_construction_reports table with log_date and status
Benchmarking Use: Compare daily log completion rate across projects and superintendents to benchmark reporting discipline
Notes: Daily log completion is the foundation for all field analytics — workforce hours, weather delays, material deliveries, safety incidents. Projects with < 80% completion are unreliable for field benchmarking.

Description: Average elapsed time from correspondence issuance to response — measures communication efficiency between project parties. Formula: Response Date − Issue Date (in business days). Tracks formal communications (owner directives, architect supplemental instructions, notices, letters).
Industry Standard: Contract-specified response periods; AIA communication provisions
Common Values: Typical: 3–10 business days depending on correspondence type
Source Systems: Derived from: correspondences (dates), correspondence_responses (dates)
Procore Coverage: High — correspondences and correspondence_responses tables with type definitions
Benchmarking Use: Compare correspondence volume and response time across project types
Notes: Correspondence types (via correspondence_types table) enable segmentation by communication category. High correspondence volume with slow response signals communication breakdown.
